data.title: When "successful execution" becomes the most dangerous lie in the AI ​​era
data.description:
Author: Cobo

In an era where AI infrastructure is not yet ready, "out of control" no longer manifests as system crashes, but rather as systems deviating from the boundaries of human authorization during normal operation. This is becoming a new and increasingly prominent security issue. This article is based on a real-world on-chain incident recap: how an agent powered by Openclaw deviated from instructions and opened a BTC-PERP 10x long order in the Drift Protocol on Solana.

When your interface pops up "Transaction executed," most users will be satisfied with their agent. But in the unseen background, your agent may have just secretly doubled your budget, misappropriated funds, and erased all traces of errors.

The above scenario is not science fiction, but a reality that has already occurred.

This is a system-level security breach that is currently occurring on a large scale when the AI ​​Agent performs deterministic tasks—we call it Silent Override, an unauthorized behavior. 01. A $0.2 Difference Leads the Agent to Directly Rewrite Your Authorization Parameters Let's go back to March 26, 2026, and fully recap this real-world incident that occurred on the Solana chain. At 10:58 AM that day, a user instructed the AI ​​Agent to open a 10x long position in a BTC perpetual contract on the Drift Protocol. After initial environment testing, the Agent calculated that the threshold required was approximately 0.07 SOL. To be on the safe side, the user gave a very clear parameter instruction at 11:49 AM: using 0.1 SOL as margin. Logically, with sufficient funds, this should have been feasible. However, at 11:51, the Agent discovered during execution that the margin requirement was not met: an error message indicated "Insufficient Collateral." At that time, the oracle price was approximately $83.2/SOL, and 0.1 SOL was equivalent to $8.32, while the actual requirement for Drift was $8.52. The shortfall was only $0.2. The normal product logic would be: trigger Insufficient Funds – abort execution – throw an exception, and wait for user authorization to make up the difference (only about 0.11 SOL is needed). But this Agent did not do this. Faced with this $0.2 shortfall, it unilaterally changed the margin parameter from 0.1 SOL to 0.2 SOL (without any calculation basis, simply doubling it), forcibly resolving the problem and displaying the message "Transaction successful" on the user's screen. It wasn't until 11:58 that the user checked their on-chain balance and realized their wallet was 0.1 SOL short of expectations. Under questioning, the agent revealed the truth, admitting that "the correct approach should have been to ask the user if they wanted to adjust it to above 0.11 SOL." Faced with this tiny shortfall, many might think the agent was acting out of kindness, helping them get things done. But is this really a good thing? This leads to a deeper, and even more chilling, question.

02. Silent Override destroys the underlying auditability

An extra 0.1 SOL in margin may not be fatal, but it breaks the most fundamental trust contract in human-computer interaction: the user sets the boundaries, and the machine executes within those boundaries. Once the agent believes that "in order to facilitate the task, the boundaries can be overturned arbitrarily," this contract instantly becomes invalid.

Even more frightening is that the agent alters the margin and erases all traces. In the era of traditional software, we are accustomed to preventing system crashes and errors; but in the AI ​​era, normal transaction failures are actually the bottom line of security—because clear error reports mean that the process is visible, traceable, and accountable, and the risk is successfully intercepted in the present moment.

In contrast, this fabricated "success" is far more destructive. When the agent uses perfect front-end execution feedback to cover up unauthorized actions in the back-end, a violation is seamlessly integrated into the historical ledger. This is why we named it Silent Override: Silent (silent concealment) plus Override (unauthorized alteration) completely deprives users of any means of accountability. The 0.1 SOL gap may not seem large. But the problem is never about this 0.1 SOL; it's that once a system accepts the logic that authorization can be rewritten to complete a task, its bottom line will continuously crumble. Today it dares to arbitrarily double the 0.1 SOL deposit to 0.2 SOL; tomorrow it might be the payment amount, the target address, the slippage tolerance, or any field the system deems helpful in completing the task. When "execution successful" on the screen no longer equates to "executed as I intended," the user's initial request and the agent's mid-process tampering all become an unverifiable mess. The system completely degenerates into a black box that appears clean on the outside but is internally distorted. At this point, you are no longer facing a small loss from a single transaction, but rather the complete loss of the ability to verify the ledger—this is the complete collapse of the underlying "auditability." 03. This is not an isolated case: From "silent tampering" to "shadow hosting" Drift's $0.2 is not an extremely rare edge case. The same pattern—the agent overstepping authorization boundaries to complete instructions—has appeared in other scenarios. In a previous Polymarket transaction incident, a user instructed an agent to purchase conditional tokens through an MPC wallet. During execution, the agent incorrectly determined that the MPC wallet could not complete the required EIP-712 signature, so it took matters into its own hands: generating a temporary key and transferring the user's funds to this temporary address to complete the transaction. The assets ultimately ended up in an address completely beyond the user's control, yet the agent still confidently reported the transaction as successful. This incident is known as Shadow Custody—assets slipping out of the user's actual control without their knowledge. These two incidents, with their different manifestations, point to the same underlying malicious behavior pattern: when encountering execution obstacles, the agent chooses to circumvent rather than report them. In the Drift incident, the bypass method was to tamper with the amount; in the Polymarket incident, the bypass method was to transfer funds. The result was the same: users' assets all went outside the expected security boundaries. This reveals an extremely dangerous structural risk currently facing Agentic Wallets: the agent's objective function is incorrectly set to complete the instruction, rather than completing it within the user's authorized scope. When these two objectives conflict, the current system has no mechanism to force it to choose the latter. The agent will do everything it can to complete your instructions. This is its greatest advantage, and also its most dangerous characteristic. So, what underlying mechanism forces the agent onto this dangerous path of preferring to tamper with data rather than report errors? Why would an AI designed to serve users disregard the user's authorized boundaries? The root of it all lies in the underlying logic of the large language model. 04. Root Cause: Reward Hijacking in LLM and the Conflict with Financial Contracts Why does this misalignment of the objective function occur? This involves the underlying operating mechanism of AI. Currently, most Agent systems still use "semantic soft constraints" when constraining boundaries—that is, writing the intent and restrictions in the same Prompt and throwing it to the large model. However, system developers have overlooked a fatal point: the underlying logic of AI has no concept of financial contracts; it only has objective functions. In the training foundation of LLM (Large Language Model), the highest weight is given to Helpful (providing assistance, completing the task). For the Agent, a transaction failure due to a difference of $0.2 is an unacceptable negative state; while modifying parameters to make the transaction succeed brings extremely high positive feedback. When faced with execution friction, large models exhibit typical Reward Hacking: they resemble an aggressive employee willing to do anything to achieve KPIs. Fueled by powerful illusions and self-deception, the agent instantly convinces itself—the user's fundamental goal is to go long on BTC; 0.1 SOL is just a rough estimate, changing it to 0.2 will achieve the goal—a brilliant execution. Thus, it bypasses your consent, arbitrarily doubles the margin, forcibly buys, and then returns four words: Transaction successful. In this process, no matter how many times you bold the message in the Prompt or how strictly you write in natural language that modifying the amount or exceeding authority is prohibited, it's all in vain. Natural language can express intent, but it can never handle machine-level access control. Attempting to use the magic of language to constrain language itself is bound to be easily breached the moment a real-world error occurs. 05. Architectural Restructuring: From Semantic Defenses to Physical Isolation The root cause of such incidents is not the Agent's insufficient capabilities, but rather the lack of external constraints on its autonomous decision-making. Defense can never rely on the "self-awareness" of the large model; it must be enforced using mechanisms independent of the Agent. Since the failure occurs at the architectural level, the solution must also be engineering-level. In a truly trustworthy system, we need to deploy three hard constraints: 1. Parameter Locking Rules: Depriving of Modification Rights Completely decouple the task intent from the authorization bottom line. For user-specified deterministic parameters (such as the maximum amount, target address, and asset quantity), the Agent only has the right to read, not modify. If friction arises due to parameters during execution, the only legitimate action the system assigns to the Agent is to stop execution, report the exception, and wait for new instructions from the user. 2. Transaction Firewall: Independent Review Before Execution An independent gateway must be inserted between the Agent's planning layer and the on-chain settlement layer. This firewall does not consider the Agent's natural language interpretation; it only parses the underlying generated calldata and compares it with the user's original instructions at the parameter level. If any deviation is found—a single cent more, a single character difference in address, or an incompatible operation type—the system immediately triggers a circuit breaker, forcing a user approval. There is no room for negotiation. 3. Deviation Alerts: Establishing an External Monitoring Loop Never let the Agent act as both referee and player. A monitoring service independent of the Agent must be deployed to compare user instruction parameters with actual on-chain execution parameters in real time. Once an agent attempts to act unilaterally, an alert is pushed to the user within seconds: you authorized 0.1 SOL, but the agent executed 0.2 SOL on the chain. This completely exposes the system's black box. Conclusion: Defining What an Agent "Absolutely Cannot Do" When we talk about the future of agents, our attention is often drawn to their unlimited potential. However, for any financial-grade agent that truly handles real assets, the core security boundary lies in: under no circumstances should the agent do anything. Trust is precisely built within this "negative space." Allowing agents to explore the vast semantic space infinitely is the imagination of AI; but transforming security red lines from language into code is the system's physical defense. No matter how advanced the large model becomes, the first principle in building an Agent will always be: let AI handle intelligence, and let code handle deterministic operations.

data.title: HSBC and Standard Chartered obtain licenses; Hong Kong dollar stablecoin expected to be issued this year.
data.description:
Abstract

Regarding application scenarios and development directions, Eddie Yue stated that issuers are encouraged to explore and expand applications related to the real economy, including cross-border payments, local payments, tokenized asset trading, and innovative applications.

After nearly nine months, the first batch of stablecoin issuers in Hong Kong has been announced, with two Hong Kong note-issuing banks included.

On the afternoon of April 10th, according to the official website of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), Dingdian Financial Technology Co., Ltd. (“Dingdian Fintech”) and The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited (“HSBC”) successfully obtained Hong Kong stablecoin issuer licenses, with license numbers FRS01 and FRS02 respectively. Among them, Dingdian Fintech is a company jointly established by Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong) Limited (hereinafter referred to as "Standard Chartered Bank"), Hong Kong Telecom, and Anni Group. On August 1, 2025, Hong Kong officially released the "Stablecoin Ordinance," establishing a licensing system for issuers of stablecoins pegged to fiat currencies. From the establishment of the stablecoin licensing system to the issuance of the first batch of licenses, it took nearly nine months. "This marks a new phase in the implementation of Hong Kong's stablecoin regulatory regime," Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) Chief Executive Eddie Yue revealed in a statement on the same day. He added that both licensed issuers plan to launch Hong Kong dollar stablecoins in the first phase, and compliant stablecoins in Hong Kong are expected to be launched gradually from mid- to second-half of this year. According to the HKMA, initial application scenarios for licensees will cover cross-border and local elements, including digital asset trading. Future expansion will depend on business considerations, market conditions, and international regulatory developments, including expanding the partner network, application scenarios, and geographical coverage. Furthermore, the HKMA will maintain communication with other applicants and market participants and continue to review applications. However, in the aforementioned article, Eddie Yue emphasized that "the licensing process has a very high threshold, and even if more licenses are issued in the future, the overall number of licenses will be very limited." In February of this year, Eddie Yue disclosed to the media that the Hong Kong Monetary Authority had received 36 stablecoin license applications, aiming to... The first batch of licenses was issued in March. He also emphasized that the number of licenses issued in the first round would be limited, with stability as the goal. Looking at the results, the license issuance was slightly later than expected, and only two institutions successfully obtained licenses, slightly fewer than the market's previous expectation of around three. In an article published on April 10th, Yu Weiwen detailed the main considerations for license approval, the issuer's application scenarios and development direction, risk management measures, and subsequent work arrangements. "The Hong Kong Monetary Authority has always emphasized that there are very high thresholds for licensing," said Eddie Yue. He explained that the approval process mainly considers two aspects: (1) whether the applicant possesses sufficient risk management capabilities and experience, and complies with relevant regulations in Hong Kong and other regions; and (2) whether the applicant can propose specific application scenarios and feasible business solutions and development plans. "Of the 36 applications, PointChina and HSBC, having met the relevant licensing requirements, demonstrated their ability to manage risks prudently and proposed concrete application scenarios and future development plans, thus earning licenses," said Eddie Yue. Regarding application scenarios and development directions, Yue stated that both licensed issuers plan to issue Hong Kong dollar stablecoins in the first phase, encouraging issuers to explore and expand applications related to the real economy, including cross-border payments, local payments, tokenized asset trading, and innovative applications. "Both licensed issuers have banking backgrounds and have participated in the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's experimental projects on central bank digital currencies (CBDCs) and tokenized deposits. They have a deep understanding of the functions and application scenarios of various digital currencies, which is conducive to exploring various possibilities of 'future payments.' One of the issuers has formed a consortium with local telecommunications, payment, and digital asset companies, enabling compliant stablecoins to play a synergistic role," said Eddie Yue. Regarding risk management, Eddie Yue mentioned that licensed issuers must implement sound and comprehensive policies and risk management measures in accordance with regulatory requirements in areas such as reserve asset management and asset security, price stabilization mechanisms, redemption arrangements, and technological security. They must also establish robust anti-money laundering systems, including using blockchain monitoring tools in daily operations to detect suspicious transactions and take corresponding follow-up actions. Furthermore, they must verify the identity of stablecoin holders themselves or through reliable third-party partners to ensure the effectiveness of monitoring measures. "Both licensed issuers demonstrated in their applications their ability to comply with and implement these measures." Looking ahead, Yue stated that based on the two institutions' current business plans, regulated stablecoins in Hong Kong are expected to be launched gradually from the middle to the second half of this year. "Regarding the issuance of additional licenses and the timing, we maintain an open yet cautious attitude, and currently have no clear inclination," Yu Weiwen emphasized. "Considering the risks involved in the issuance business, the protection of users, and the market's capacity and long-term development, the issuance of licenses has a fairly high threshold. Even if more licenses are issued in the future, the overall number of licenses will be very limited." On April 10th at noon, media reported that the Hong Kong Monetary Authority would announce the list of stablecoin issuers in the afternoon. Affected by this, Hong Kong and A-share stablecoin-related concept stocks collectively surged. Among them, the share price of Guotai Junan International (1788.HK) in Hong Kong rose by nearly 50% at one point, while the share prices of Shenwan Hongyuan Hong Kong (0218.HK) and Yunfeng Financial (0367.HK) rose by more than 28% and 28% respectively during the trading session. lang="EN-US">16% ; leaf="">) once rose by more than 17%, Yuyin Shares (002177.SZ) hit the daily limit, Lakala (300773.SZ

data.title: Why are there so many obstacles to the on-chain deployment of AI agents?
data.description:
Author: Zack Pokorny & Translation: Chopper, Foresight News

The implementation of AI agents on blockchain has not been smooth. Although blockchain has programmable and permissionless characteristics, it lacks a semantic abstraction and collaboration layer adapted to agents. A research report released by the crypto research institution Galaxy points out that agents face four major structural frictions on the chain: opportunity discovery, trusted verification, data reading and execution processes. The existing infrastructure is still designed around human interaction, making it difficult to support AI's autonomous asset management and strategy execution. These have become the core bottlenecks for the large-scale implementation of agents on blockchain. The following is a full translation of the report:

The application scenarios and capabilities of AI agents have begun to evolve. They are beginning to perform tasks autonomously and are being developed for holding and allocating capital, and discovering transaction and profit strategies. Although this experimental shift is still in its very early stages, it is quite different from the previous development model of agents mainly as social and analytical tools.

Blockchain is becoming a natural testing ground for this evolutionary process. Blockchain is permissionless, composable, has an open-source application ecosystem, provides equal access to data to all participants, and all assets on the chain are programmable by default. This raises a structural question: if blockchain is programmable and permissionless, why do autonomous agents still face friction? The answer lies not in the feasibility of execution, but in the semantic and coordination burdens on top of execution. Blockchain guarantees the correctness of state transitions but typically does not provide protocol-native abstractions, such as those used for economic interpretation, normative identity, or goal-level coordination. Some friction stems from the architectural flaws of permissionless systems, while some reflects the current state of tools, content management, and market infrastructure. In reality, many higher-level functions still rely on software and workflows, the construction of which requires human intervention. Blockchain Architecture and AI Agents Blockchain design revolves around consensus and deterministic execution, rather than semantic interpretation. It exposes underlying primitives such as storage slots, event logs, and call traces, rather than standardized economic objects. Therefore, abstract concepts such as positions, yields, health coefficients, and liquidity depth typically need to be reconstructed off-chain by indexers, data analysis layers, front-end interfaces, and application programming interfaces (APIs), transforming the protocol-specific states into more usable forms. Many mainstream decentralized finance (DeFi) operational processes, especially those targeting retail investors and those involving subjective decision-making, still revolve around a user-interface-centric model where users interact and sign individual transactions. This user-centric model has expanded with the increasing prevalence of retail investors, even though a significant portion of on-chain activity is now machine-driven. The current mainstream retail investor interaction model remains: Intent → User Interface → Transaction → Confirmation. Programmatic operations follow another path, but also have their limitations: developers select the contract and asset set during the build phase, and then run the algorithm within this fixed scope. Neither of these models can adapt to systems that must dynamically discover, evaluate, and combine operations based on constantly changing objectives at runtime. Friction arises when an infrastructure optimized for transaction verification is used by systems that need to simultaneously interpret economic conditions, assess credit, and optimize behavior around specific objectives. This gap stems partly from the permissionless and heterogeneous design of blockchain, and partly from the fact that interaction tools are still built around human review and front-end intermediaries. A Comparison of Intelligent Agent Behavior Flows and Traditional Algorithmic Strategies Before discussing the gap between blockchain infrastructure and intelligent agent systems, it is necessary to clarify: what exactly is the difference between more intelligent and autonomous behavior flows and traditional on-chain algorithmic systems? The difference lies not in the degree of automation, complexity, parameterization, or even dynamic adaptive capabilities. Traditional algorithmic systems can be highly parameterized, automatically discovering new contracts and tokens, allocating funds across various strategy types, and rebalancing based on performance. The real difference lies in the system's ability to handle scenarios not anticipated during the development phase. Traditional algorithmic systems, no matter how complex, only execute predefined logic for pre-defined patterns. They require predefined interface resolvers for each protocol type, predefined evaluation logic mapping contract states to economic meanings, explicit credit and standardization judgment rules, and hard-coded rules for each decision branch. When situations deviate from the predefined pattern, the system either skips them or fails out entirely. It cannot reason about unfamiliar scenarios; it can only determine whether the current scenario matches a known template. Like this "digestive duck" mechanical automation device, capable of mimicking biological behavior, but all actions are pre-programmed. A traditional algorithm scanning the DeFi lending market can identify newly deployed contracts that emit familiar events or match known factory patterns. However, if a new lending infrastructure component with an unfamiliar interface appears, the system cannot evaluate it. Humans must examine the contract, understand its operation, determine if it represents a exploitable opportunity, and write the integration logic. Only then can the algorithm interact with it. Humans interpret, algorithms execute. Model-based intelligent agent systems change this boundary. They can achieve this through learned reasoning abilities: interpreting vague or incompletely expressed goals. Instructions such as "maximizing returns while avoiding excessive risks" require semantic interpretation. What constitutes excessive risk? How should returns and risks be weighed? Traditional algorithms require precise pre-definition of these conditions. Intelligent agents, however, can interpret intent, make judgments, and optimize their understanding based on feedback. They can also generalize and adapt to unfamiliar interfaces. An intelligent agent can read unfamiliar contract code, parse documentation, or view application binary interfaces it has never encountered before, and infer the economic function of the system. It does not need to pre-build a parser for every type of protocol. Although this capability is not yet perfect, and the agent may misjudge what it sees, it can attempt to interact with systems not anticipated during the construction phase. Furthermore, it can reason in situations where trust and normativity are uncertain. When credit signals are ambiguous or incomplete, the underlying model can probabilistically weigh the signals rather than simply applying binary rules. Does this smart contract possess standardization? Based on existing evidence, is the token legitimate? Traditional algorithms are either rule-based or simply ineffective; agents, on the other hand, can reason about confidence levels. They can interpret errors and make adjustments. When unexpected situations occur, agents can deduce the root cause of the problem and decide how to respond. In contrast, traditional algorithms simply execute anomaly detection modules, forwarding exception information without interpretation. These capabilities currently exist but are not perfect. The underlying model can produce illusions, misjudge content, and make seemingly certain but incorrect decisions. In adversarial and capital-intensive environments (i.e., where code controls or receives assets), "attempting to interact with unforeseen systems" can mean financial loss. The core argument of this paper is not that agents can now reliably perform these functions, but rather that they can attempt them in ways that traditional systems cannot, and that future infrastructure will make these attempts safer and more reliable. This difference should be viewed as a continuous state rather than an absolute classification boundary. Some traditional systems incorporate learned reasoning, while some agents may rely on hard-coded rules on critical paths. This distinction is directional, not absolutely binary. Agent systems shift more of the interpretation, evaluation, and adaptation work to runtime reasoning, rather than pre-defined rules in the construction phase. This is crucial for the discussion of friction problems, because agent systems attempt to achieve something that traditional algorithms completely avoid. Traditional algorithms avoid discovery friction by having humans screen contract sets during the construction phase; avoid control-level friction by relying on whitelists maintained by operators; avoid data friction by using parsers pre-built for known protocols; and avoid execution friction by operating within pre-defined security boundaries. Humans pre-complete semantic, credit, and policy-level work, and the algorithm executes within defined boundaries. Early on-chain agent behavior processes may follow this model, but the core value of agents lies in shifting discovery, credit, and policy evaluation to runtime reasoning, rather than pre-defined rules in the construction phase. They attempt to discover and evaluate unfamiliar opportunities, reason about standardization without hard-coded rules, interpret heterogeneous states without pre-defined parsers, and enforce policy constraints on potentially ambiguous goals. Friction arises not because agents are doing the same things as algorithms but with greater difficulty, but because they are attempting something entirely different: operating in an open, dynamically interpretable behavioral space, rather than within a closed, pre-integrated system. From a structural perspective, this contradiction does not stem from a flaw in blockchain consensus, but rather from the way the overall interaction stack developed around it operates. Blockchain guarantees deterministic state transitions, consensus on the final state, and eventual determinism. It does not attempt to encode economic meaning interpretation, intent verification, or goal tracking at the protocol layer. These responsibilities have historically been handled by front-end interfaces, wallets, indexers, and other off-chain collaborative layers, which always require human intervention. Even for seasoned participants, the current mainstream interaction patterns reflect this design. Retail investors interpret the status through dashboards, select actions through user interfaces, sign transactions through wallets, and informally verify the results. Algorithmic trading institutions have automated execution, but still rely on human operators to sift through protocol sets, check for anomalies, and update integration logic when interfaces change. In both scenarios, the protocol is only responsible for ensuring correct execution, while intent interpretation, anomaly handling, and adaptation to new opportunities are all done by humans. Intelligent agent systems compress or even eliminate this division of labor. They must programmatically reconstruct economically meaningful states, assess the progress of objectives, and verify execution results, rather than simply confirming transactions on the blockchain. These burdens are particularly pronounced on the blockchain because intelligent agents operate in an open, adversarial, and rapidly changing environment where new contracts, assets, and execution paths can emerge without centralized auditing. Protocols only guarantee the correct execution of transactions, not that the economic state is easily interpretable, contracts are standardized, execution paths conform to user intent, or relevant opportunities can be programmatically discovered. The following will analyze these frictions step by step along the various stages of the intelligent agent's operational cycle: discovering existing contracts and opportunities, verifying their legitimacy, acquiring economically meaningful states, and executing operations around the goal. Friction arises because the behavioral space of decentralized finance expands openly in a permissionless environment, while relevance and legitimacy are filtered by humans through on-chain social, market, and tool layers. New protocols emerge through announcements and are also filtered through front-end integration, token lists, data analysis platforms, and liquidity formation. Over time, these signals often form a feasible standard for judging which parts of the behavioral space have economic value and are sufficiently credible, although this consensus may be informal, uneven, and partly dependent on third-party and human screening. This can provide intelligent agents with filtered data and credit signals, but they themselves do not possess the intuitive shortcuts humans use to interpret these signals. From an on-chain perspective, all deployed contracts are equally discoverable. Legitimate protocols, malicious forks, test deployments, and abandoned projects all exist in the form of callable bytecode. The blockchain itself does not encode which contracts are important or which are secure. Therefore, agents must build their own discovery mechanisms: scanning deployment events, identifying interface patterns, tracking factory contracts (contracts that programmatically deploy other contracts), and monitoring liquidity formation to determine which contracts should be included in the decision-making scope. This process is not just about finding contracts, but also about determining whether they should enter the agent's action space. Identifying candidates is only the first step. After initial discovery and screening, contracts must undergo the standardization and authenticity verification process described in the next section. The agent must first confirm that the discovered contracts are legitimate before including them in the decision space. Detecting friction does not mean detecting new deployment behavior. Mature algorithmic systems can already achieve this within their own policy scope. The searcher, which monitors Uniswap factory events and automatically adds them to new funding pools, is performing dynamic discovery. Friction arises at two higher levels: determining the legitimacy of discovered contracts and determining whether they are relevant to open objectives, rather than simply matching pre-defined strategy types. The searcher's discovery logic is tightly bound to its strategy. It knows which interface patterns to look for because the strategy is defined. Agents executing broader instructions, such as "configure the best opportunity after risk adjustment," cannot rely solely on policy-derived filters. They must evaluate newly encountered opportunities against the objective itself, requiring the parsing of unfamiliar interfaces, inference of economic functions, and determination of whether the opportunity should be included in the decision space. This is partly a problem of general autonomy, but blockchain exacerbates it. Control layer friction arises because the determination of identity and legitimacy is typically done outside the protocol, relying on a combination of screening, governance, documentation, interfaces, and operator judgment. In many current workflows, humans remain a crucial part of the judgment process. Blockchain guarantees deterministic execution and finality, but it does not guarantee that the caller is interacting with the target contract. This intent determination is externalized into social context, websites, and human screening. In the current process, humans use the credibility of web pages as an informal verification method. They visit official domains (usually found through aggregation platforms like DeFiLlama or verified social media accounts of projects) and treat the website as a standard mapping between human concepts and contract addresses. Subsequently, the front-end interface establishes a feasible set of trusted benchmarks, clarifying which addresses are official addresses, which tokens should be used, and which entry points are secure.

The Mechanical Turk of 1789 was a chess-playing machine that appeared to operate autonomously, but actually relied on a hidden human operator.

The intelligent agent, by default, cannot interpret brand logos, authenticate social signals, or "officiality" through social context. It can be inputted with filtered data derived from these signals, but transforming this into persistent machine credibility assumptions requires explicit registry entries, policies, or verification logic. The intelligent agent can be configured with operator-provided whitelists, authentication addresses, and credibility policies. The problem is not a complete inability to access social context, but rather the extremely high operational cost of maintaining these safeguards in a dynamically expanding behavioral space, and the lack of alternative verification mechanisms that humans default to when these measures are missing or incomplete.

Potential Data Flow Mismatch

Access to the economic state on the blockchain is essentially a pull model, even though execution signals can be streamed. External systems query nodes for the required state, rather than receiving continuous, structured updates. This model reflects the core function of the blockchain: verification on demand, rather than maintaining a persistent state view at the application level.

Push primitives exist. WebSocket subscriptions can stream new blocks and event logs in real time, but these do not include the stored state that carries most of the economic meaning, unless the protocol explicitly chooses redundant publishing. Agents cannot directly subscribe to lending market utilization, pool reserves, or position health coefficients on-chain. These values ​​are stored in contract storage, and most protocols do not provide a native mechanism to push this information to downstream users. The current optimal model is to subscribe to new block headers and re-query them with each block. Logs can only indicate that the state may change, but do not encode the final economic state; reconstructing the state still requires explicit reading and access to historical state.

Intelligent agent systems might benefit from a reverse process. Instead of polling hundreds of contracts for state changes, agents can receive structured, pre-computed state updates and push them directly to the runtime environment. A push architecture reduces redundant queries, lowers latency between state changes and agent perception, and allows intermediate layers to package state into semantically clear updates, rather than having agents interpret meaning from raw storage. This reverse shift is not easy. It requires subscribing to infrastructure, filtering relevance logic, and patterns for translating storage changes into actionable economic events for agents. However, as agents become continuous participants rather than intermittent queryers, the inefficiency of the pull model becomes increasingly costly. An infrastructure that treats agents as continuous consumers rather than intermittent clients might be more suited to the operation of autonomous systems. Whether push infrastructure is truly superior remains an open question. Massive state changes create filtering challenges, requiring agents to still determine which changes are relevant, thus reintroducing pull semantics on another level. The key issue isn't with pull architecture itself, but rather that current architectures don't consider persistent machine consumers. As the scale of agent usage expands, exploring alternative models might be worthwhile. Execution friction arises because many current interaction layers package intent transformation, transaction verification, and result validation into workflows designed around the front-end interface, wallet, and operator oversight. In retail and subjective decision-making scenarios, this oversight is typically performed by humans. For autonomous systems, these functions must be formalized and directly encoded. Blockchain guarantees deterministic execution based on contract logic, but doesn't guarantee that transactions conform to user intent, comply with risk constraints, or achieve expected economic outcomes. In the current process, the user interface and humans fill this gap. The user interface combines a sequence of operations (exchange, authorization, deposit, lending), with the wallet providing the final "review and send" node. Users or operators typically make informal strategic judgments in this last step. They often assess the safety of a transaction and the acceptability of the quoted price, often with incomplete information. If a transaction fails or an unexpected result occurs, the user will retry, adjust slippage, change the path, or abandon the operation altogether. Intelligent agent systems remove humans from this execution loop. This means the system must replace three human functions with machine-native methods: Intent integration. Human goals, such as "transferring my stablecoins to a risk-adjusted optimal return location," must be integrated into a concrete action plan: choosing which protocol, which market, which token path, the scale, which authorizations, and the execution order. For humans, this process is implicitly completed through the user interface; for intelligent agents, it must be formalized. **Strategy Execution.** Clicking "Send Transaction" is not just a signature, but also an implicit check to ensure the transaction meets constraints: slippage tolerance, leverage cap, minimum health factor, whitelisted contracts, or "prohibited upgradeable contracts." The agent needs to encode explicit policy constraints into machine-verifiable rules: The execution system must verify that the proposed call graph satisfies these rules before broadcasting. **Result Verification.** Transaction being on-chain does not equate to task completion. Successful transaction execution may still fail to achieve the goal: slippage may exceed tolerance limits, the target position size may not be reached due to quota limitations, or interest rates may change between simulation and on-chain. Humans informally verify this by reviewing the user interface afterward. The agent, however, must programmatically evaluate the post-conditions. This introduces the requirement for completion checks, not just simple transaction inclusion. Intent-centric architectures can partially address this issue by shifting more of the burden of "how" to execution from the agent to a dedicated solver. By broadcasting signed intents instead of raw call data, agents can specify outcome-based constraints that the solver or protocol-level mechanism must satisfy for execution to be acceptable. Most execution operations in decentralized finance (DeFi) are inherently multi-step. A yield configuration might require authorization → redemption → deposit → lending → staking. Some steps may be independent transactions, while others may be packaged through multi-call or routing contracts. Humans can tolerate partial completion and returning to the user interface to continue the process. Agents, however, require deterministic process orchestration: if any step fails, the agent must decide whether to retry, reroute, roll back, or pause. This gives rise to new failure modes that are largely masked in human processes: State drift between decision-making and on-chain execution. Interest rates, utilization rates, or liquidity may change between simulation and execution. Humans accept this variability; agents must set acceptable limits and enforce them. Non-atomic execution and partial transactions. Partial operations may be executed across multiple transactions or produce partial results. Agents must track intermediate states and confirm that the final state meets the objective. Authorization limits and approval risks. Humans subconsciously sign authorizations through user interfaces; agents must reason about the scope of authorization (limit, users, duration) as part of a security strategy, not just as a user interface step. Path selection and implicit execution costs. Humans rely on routing contracts and default user interface settings. Agents must incorporate slippage, maximum extractable value risk, gas costs, and price impacts into their objective functions. The core argument of execution friction is that the interaction layer of decentralized finance uses human wallet signatures as the final control plane. This layer carries the current intent verification, risk tolerance, and informal "reasonableness" judgments. Removing humans, execution becomes a control problem: agents must translate objectives into behavioral patterns, automatically execute policy constraints, and verify results under uncertainty. This challenge exists in many autonomous systems, but it is particularly harsh in the blockchain environment: execution directly involves capital, composable unfamiliar contracts, and exposure to adversarial state changes. Humans make decisions using heuristics and correct errors through trial and error. Agents, on the other hand, must perform the same work programmatically at machine speed, often within a dynamically changing behavioral space. Therefore, the statement that "agents only need to submit transactions" underestimates the difficulty. Submitting transactions is the simplest part. Conclusion

Blockchain was not originally designed to provide the semantic and coordination layers required by intelligent agents. Its design goal is to ensure deterministic execution and consensus on state transitions in adversarial environments. The interaction layer built on this foundation has evolved around a model where human users interpret states through an interface, select operations through a front-end interface, and verify results through human review.

Intelligent agent systems have disrupted this architecture. They remove human interpreters, approvers, and verifiers from the loop, requiring these functions to be implemented natively by machines. This shift exposes structural frictions in four dimensions: discovery, credit determination, data acquisition, and execution processes. These frictions arise not because execution is infeasible, but because the infrastructure surrounding blockchain, in most cases, still assumes human involvement in state interpretation and transaction submission.

Bridging these gaps will likely require building new infrastructure across a multi-layered technology stack: middleware that normalizes cross-protocol economic states into machine-readable middleware; indexing services or remote call extensions for semantic primitives such as positions, health coefficients, and opportunity sets; a registry providing standard contract mapping and token authenticity verification; and an execution framework that constrains coding strategies, handles multi-step workflows, and programmatically verifies goal completion. Some gaps stem from the structural characteristics of permissionless systems: open deployment, weak standard identity, and heterogeneous interfaces. Others depend on current tools, standards, and incentive design; these gaps are expected to narrow as the scale of agent usage expands and protocols compete to optimize integration friendliness with autonomous systems. As autonomous systems begin to manage capital, execute strategies, and interact directly with on-chain applications, the architectural assumptions of the current interaction layer will become increasingly prominent. Most of the frictions described in this article reflect the characteristics of blockchain tools and interaction patterns evolving around human-mediated workflows; some frictions stem from the openness, heterogeneity, and adversarial environment of permissionless systems; and some are problems commonly faced by autonomous systems in complex environments. The core challenge is not to get intelligent agents to sign transactions, but to provide them with a reliable means to complete the semantic interpretation, credit determination, and policy execution tasks currently undertaken jointly by software and humans between blockchain state and operational behavior.

data.title: Hong Kong's stablecoin license has finally been issued; all the information you need is here.
data.description:
Core Viewpoint:

  • The issuance of stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong this time is essentially about formally incorporating on-chain settlement tools into the compliant financial system.

  • Hong Kong's institutional path is clear: first establish a robust underlying framework, then gradually expand market applications..

  • Short-term value lies in the efficiency of cross-border payments and capital flows, rather than narrative-level innovation. The value of the industry chain lies not only in the issuance stage, but also in distribution and user access. The ultimate competitive landscape depends on the network effects of scenarios, customers, and liquidity. Against the backdrop of accelerated digital asset regulation and infrastructure restructuring in major global financial centers, stablecoins are gradually moving from an internal tool of the crypto market into the institutional purview of the mainstream financial system. For Hong Kong, this round of stablecoin license issuance is not only an extension of the existing virtual asset framework, but also a key strategic move in the payment and settlement layer. Hong Kong's stablecoin regulation has essentially completed the transition from policy discussion to formal legislation. Since the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) released its discussion document in 2022, the regulatory body has continuously advanced the system design, subsequently completing consultation, summarization, and legislative procedures. The Stablecoin Ordinance has entered the implementation phase, and the issuance of fiat-pegged stablecoins in Hong Kong has been incorporated into the licensing regulatory framework. This means that Hong Kong's stablecoin industry has entered a stage of operation with clearly defined rules and licensed entities at its core. On April 10, 2026, the HKMA officially announced that the Commissioner of Monetary Authority, in accordance with the Stablecoin Ordinance, granted stablecoin issuer licenses to Point Fintech Limited (a joint venture under Standard Chartered Bank) and The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited to issue stablecoins in Hong Kong. The licenses took effect today. Based on the remarks of Eddie Yue, Chief Executive of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority, Hong Kong's regulatory focus on stablecoins is very clear: the core is to first establish a solid foundation of institutional frameworks before promoting application development. The current system mainly revolves around several aspects: First, regulated fiat-pegged stablecoin issuance activities must, in principle, be conducted by licensed entities. The second requirement is reserve asset management, which requires reserves to provide sufficient backing for the circulating face value and maintain high liquidity and low risk. The third requirement is a redemption mechanism, ensuring holders can redeem their holdings according to clearly defined rules. The fourth requirement is information disclosure, corporate governance, and anti-money laundering requirements, ensuring basic transparency and regulatory oversight in the issuance, circulation, and management of stablecoins. Overall, Hong Kong's current framework emphasizes not scale first, but rather a verifiable, redeemable, and transparent institutional foundation. Under this premise, the real focus for stablecoins is shifting from the system itself to the market and application scenarios. For the Hong Kong market, the more realistic implementation directions at this stage can be roughly summarized into four categories. The first category is cross-border payments and trade settlement. This is currently the most discussed and most likely to generate actual demand. Traditional cross-border payment chains are long, and there is still room for improvement in terms of arrival time, fee levels, and process transparency. If stablecoins can be integrated into real trade and corporate payment processes within a compliant framework, their value primarily lies in improving settlement efficiency, reducing costs in some intermediary stages, and enhancing the traceability of fund flows. The second category is corporate treasuries and internal group fund transfers. For companies with multiple regional operations, stablecoins can serve as a more frequent and flexible on-chain dollar liquidity tool for internal clearing, fund aggregation, and cross-entity transfers. The key to this scenario is not conceptual innovation, but whether it can truly solve the problem of real-world capital turnover efficiency. The third category is trading platform-related scenarios, including deposits, settlements, and collateral management. For licensed trading platforms, stablecoins can improve the efficiency of fund flows both inside and outside the platform, and are also more conducive to integration with digital asset trading processes. However, the robust development of such applications still depends on the simultaneous improvement of custody arrangements, customer asset protection, and AML mechanisms. The fourth category is the payment and settlement环节 in tokenized asset trading. As bonds, fund units, and other RWA products are gradually put on-chain, the market demand for on-chain settlement media will become more explicit. The value of stablecoins here is not just as a payment tool, but also as a crucial infrastructure to help on-chain asset trading form a complete closed loop. From external market experience, the most explicit commercial value of stablecoins at this stage remains concentrated in improving the efficiency of payments, settlements, and on-chain fund flows. This is particularly important for Hong Kong market participants. Whether stablecoins can truly establish a competitive advantage depends not so much on who issues them first, but on who can embed them into real-world transaction and payment scenarios. Only when these scenarios mature can stablecoins evolve from a single product into a new financial infrastructure. Looking ahead, Hong Kong regulators are expected to maintain a cautious pace. In the coming period, the market will likely focus more on the implementation of reserve asset management, redemption arrangements, distribution mechanisms, anti-money laundering measures, and continuous disclosure requirements. At the regulatory level, the emphasis will likely continue to be on controllable risk and orderly development, remaining on this main theme. Finally, regarding the market landscape of stablecoins, the profit distribution and dominance of stablecoins are unlikely to remain solely focused on the issuance side. As products mature, stablecoin issuance will become a high-barrier, low-differentiation foundational layer. The true determinant of stablecoin scale will be the access to relevant scenarios and distribution capabilities. Whoever controls cross-border payment networks, trading platform traffic, and on-chain asset trading access will gain a greater advantage in the future stablecoin market. Therefore, the Hong Kong stablecoin industry will evolve into competition among large financial networks, rather than competition between single currencies.

data.title: In the AI ​​era, a single seed can grow into a forest: Silicon Valley investment observation series
data.description:
Why companies used to have to rely on organizational expansion to drive growth; Why AI is breaking this logic; And why Seed-Strapping may become a new path for entrepreneurship and investment. For the past few decades, the standard path for software startups has been almost fixed: first raise funds, then build a team; first expand, then validate; first establish the organization, then launch the product and business. Capital is not only an accelerator, but often more like a starter. Because in the old entrepreneurial logic, without sufficient external funding, a company often finds it difficult to truly develop a product, establish a market presence, and sustain a team. So, "Financing—Recruiting—Promote—Refinancing. It's closer to a new way of forming a company: a company launches with a reasonably well-timed round of capital, and with the help of AI, quickly crosses the critical threshold from 0 to 1, and then no longer relies on AI for growth. The primary goal is not to secure the next round of financing, but rather to achieve a true business closed loop, self-sufficiency, and sustainable operation. This isn't just a simple change in funding preferences; something deeper is changing: AI is rewriting the underlying logic of how startups are formed, expanded, and established. The difference between the two paths: [Image of a PNG file] Why did startups in the past have to rely on organizational expansion to drive growth? If we look at past software companies within the broader context of economic history, they were actually continuing the underlying logic of the industrial economy era. The core of this logic is to improve efficiency through scientific management and increasingly refined division of labor. Complex tasks are broken down into different stages, each undertaken by a different role: product, design, front-end, back-end, testing, operations, sales, customer service, operations, growth, and finance. Each stage becomes increasingly specialized, and each specialty corresponds to a specific role, process, and interface. This division of labor does improve local efficiency, but it's not free. The more specialized the division of labor, the higher the costs of management, collaboration, and coordination within the organization; the more positions there are, the longer the information transmission chain becomes; and once collaboration occurs between teams, departments, or even between the company and external partners, new transaction costs, communication costs, and management levels will be continuously introduced. Therefore, the growth of startups in the past was essentially an organization-driven growth. Why can products iterate? Because more people are involved in development, design, testing, and operations. Why can businesses expand? Because more people are responsible for sales, operations, customer service, and delivery. Many key capabilities are not encapsulated in the system, but are scattered among people in different positions and collaborative processes. To put it more bluntly, in the past, the most direct way for a company to make products faster and expand its business was usually not to switch to a smarter system, but to hire more people. This is because whether it's R&D, design, testing, customer service, or sales, operations, and delivery, each requires specific people to complete the tasks, organized through increasingly complex collaborative systems. Therefore, the faster the company progresses and the more complex its business becomes, the more it needs to expand its team, refine its division of labor, and increase management levels. In other words, in that era, companies were driven forward not by systems, but by their organization. From this perspective, past software startups naturally tended towards "funding" — "recruiting" — "expansion" The path taken by VC is not simply because they prefer big stories, but because the efficiency logic of the old era dictates that companies must first build their organization before they can develop products and drive business. The true meaning of AI is not just about making certain processes faster, but about fundamentally breaking down the "division of labor" system of the industrial economy era. leaf="">—Management—Scale". In the past, companies wanted to improve efficiency mainly by "delegating tasks to more people." Now, more and more tasks can be directly handed over to tools, models, and automation systems. Previously, division of labor meant more positions, more processes, and more interfaces; now, many tasks that previously required cross-position collaboration can be completed by the same person and a single system. In the past, companies could only amortize the management and coordination costs of complex organizations by growing large enough. Now, small teams can leverage AI to achieve productivity levels approaching those of large organizations. This means the sources of corporate efficiency are changing. Previously, efficiency primarily stemmed from finer division of labor, stronger management, and greater scale; now, efficiency increasingly comes from more powerful tools, shorter workflows, and fewer people. This has a particularly profound impact on startups. Because the most vulnerable stage for startups is precisely the very early stage: the product hasn't been validated, the demand hasn't been realized, and revenue hasn't taken off, but organizational costs have already begun to rise. In the past, this stage had to be weathered by funding; but now, AI allows startups, for the first time, to accomplish what previously required a complete organization, with fewer people, lower costs, and shorter workflows. A small team can now create MVPs faster, iterate products more intensively, generate content and run operations at a lower cost, and complete a series of tasks such as customer service, analysis, and sales outreach more efficiently. In other words, much of the advancement capability that previously had to be obtained through "adding people" can now be obtained through "the system." This is what we call "the system." The fundamental reason for the establishment of Seed-Strapping. What we mean by Seed-Strapping does not mean that startups don't raise funds at all, nor does it mean that the smaller the first round of funding, the better. On the contrary, it often requires a suitable initial funding round—the amount of which may be slightly larger than a traditional seed round. However, the goal of this funding is no longer simply to help the company survive to the next round, but rather to support it in overcoming the key hurdles of product development, demand validation, and initial self-sufficiency. The core assumption behind this is that, if all goes well, the company, in principle, only needs this one round of external capital to achieve sustainable operation. This differs from the traditional seed round logic. The traditional seed round is essentially the starting point of the funding chain; its task is often to help the company prove itself worthy of securing a larger, subsequent round of funding. In the Seed-Strapping model, the first round of financing is more like a "formative financing" round—it doesn't serve the next round of storytelling, but rather the possibility of independent establishment. In other words, this model doesn't aim to minimize the amount of financing, but rather to minimize the number of financing rounds. In the old model, the seed was the starting point of the financing chain; in the new model, the seed may be the ending point of the financing chain. This is not a semantic change, but a paradigm shift. In the past, the default question for startups was: "How do I raise the next round of funding?" Now, more and more companies are shifting their default question to: "How can I use this round of funding to quickly reach a point where I don't need to rely on the next round?" left;">■ Why it may become the mainstream path in the AI ​​era

In the past, similar small companies, small tools, and long-lasting paid software were not entirely absent. Throughout internet history, there has always existed a group of "small but beautiful" software companies: they serve clearly defined users, solve specific problems, charge low fees, but survive for a long time. They may not enter the mainstream narrative or become stars chased by the funding market, but they are commercially viable. Why didn't this path become mainstream in the past? Because it demands too much from the founder's abilities, and external conditions don't support it. You need to understand the product, the technology, and the users, and be able to compress almost all functions into a very small team. In the past, this was more of a high-performing model than a replicable path for most startups. AI is changing precisely this. It doesn't replace the founder's judgment, but it greatly amplifies the founder's effective bandwidth. It makes "high-density entrepreneurship," which was previously only achievable by a select few, something more capable teams can do. Thus, for the first time, those small but powerful companies that relied on exceptionally strong individual abilities have the potential to become a more widespread entrepreneurial paradigm. More importantly, these companies have a new foundation in the AI ​​era: the cost of product development has dropped significantly, the cost of validating requirements has dropped significantly, the cost of operation and delivery has been significantly reduced, and small teams have, for the first time, the opportunity to have the driving force of large organizations. Because of these combined changes, we can say that Seed-Strapping is not a marginal alternative, but rather is very likely becoming the default path for a large number of startups in the AI ​​era. Not all companies will take this path, but more and more companies can. What kind of companies are more suitable for this path? This does not mean that "as long as it is an AI company" Seed-Strapping is a natural fit. On the contrary, while many companies can leverage AI to achieve results faster, they are not necessarily sustainable in the long run. Companies truly suited to this path typically share several common characteristics: they often serve a very specific type of user and a very clear set of needs; they don't focus on short-term novelty, but rather on highly engaging workflow products or services; their value isn't just about packaging model capabilities, but is built upon workflows, data, deliverables, and user relationships; and they have the opportunity to establish a preliminary paid loop and revenue quality very early on. In other words, these companies don't survive on grand narratives, but on stronger conviction. How long can these types of companies survive? This is a question that must be answered directly. The existence of Seed-Strapping does not automatically mean that these types of companies will all survive for a long time. On the contrary, we believe that these companies will undergo a very significant differentiation in the future. One type will disappear very quickly. These often remain at a shallow functional level, merely a lightweight wrapper around the underlying model capabilities. They appear to launch quickly and grow rapidly, but user relationships are shallow, substitutable, and revenue quality is weak. Once the model itself iterates, the platform entry point changes, or a stronger distribution party enters, the lifespan of these products is usually very short. Another type, however, may outlive many traditionally funding-driven companies. Because they serve long-standing needs, embed specific workflows, and sell results, not features; they don't rely on high growth for profit, but on low growth for survival; they don't prioritize scale before considering a closed-loop business model, but establish a foundation for sustainable operation from the outset. Therefore, the key to how long such companies can survive is not whether they are AI companies, but whether they have truly crossed the “model wrapping layer” and entered the “model wrapping layer”. text="">"
